The forecast window is narrow, spanning only 34 days from May 12 to June 15, 2025. As of early May 2025, leader-level communication between Trump and Xi appears stalled. While they held an officially acknowledged call in January shortly before Trump's inauguration, recent attempts at communication have been clouded by diplomatic discord. In late April, Trump claimed a phone call had taken place regarding tariffs, which China explicitly denied. The resolution criteria specifically require both nations to acknowledge the call, meaning unilateral claims by Trump will not suffice.

Additionally, while there have been reports of a potential "birthday summit" in mid-June, Chinese officials have expressed wariness, preferring deeply scripted encounters and demanding "ground rules" be established by lower-level officials first. It was reported in late March that agreeing to a meeting could take "months." If the summit is delayed, a preparatory phone call is also likely to be pushed back. Even if the summit proceeds, standard diplomatic practice could see envoys handling the groundwork without the leaders needing to speak on the phone right before the in-person meeting. Given China's demonstrated willingness to publicly deny unscripted contact and the slow pace of diplomatic alignment, the likelihood of a mutually acknowledged phone call within this specific short window is assessed to be moderately low.
